channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Can the N.F.L. Win Fans in South Korea With Flag Football?
item.description: The inclusion of flag football in the next Olympic Games has spurred optimism about the prospects of American football in South Korea, where it has languished for decades.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim and Chang W. Lee
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: The size of South Korea’s football fandom is a mystery, even to the N.F.L. A common estimate is 150,000, the number of subscribers to a popular local football YouTuber.

item.title: Member of Neo-Nazi Party Reaches Mayoral Runoff in German Town
item.description: Since the fall of the Third Reich, German voters have shunned mayoral candidates from neo-Nazi parties. That could change on Sunday in a small town in the country’s east.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:credit: Lena Mucha for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard for the mayoral candidate Stefan Hartung hangs over the main square of Aue-Bad Schlema, which is at the heart of Germany’s debate over immigration.

item.title: A President, His Prime Minister and the Bitter Rift Dividing Senegal
item.description: The two men became president and prime minister by defeating the political old guard in Senegal. Now they are fighting each other.
item.dc:creator: Saikou Jammeh and Ruth Maclean
item.media:description: Ousmane Sonko, left, was appointed prime minister by his friend, President Bassirou Diomaye Faye, and then fired.

item.title: In Venezuela, the Debt Collector Is the Devil
item.description: Got a debt to collect in Venezuela’s capital? Dr. Diablo and his pitchfork-wielding posse use public humiliation to pressure people into paying up.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:description: Rodrigo Herrera and his team confronting a debtor last month in Caracas, Venezuela’s capital.

item.title: Pope Leo, After Taking on Trump and A.I., Prepares for New Duels in Spain
item.description: Leo arrived in Spain on Saturday for a weeklong visit in which he will meet with migrants and may come under fire from Vox, Spain’s far-right party.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV at the Royal Palace in Madrid on Saturday, as he begins a visit to Spain.

item.title: Ukraine Strikes St. Petersburg in Long-Range Drone Attack
item.description: It was the second such attack in days and came just hours after President Vladimir V. Putin spoke at an economic forum in the city.
item.dc:creator: Neil MacFarquhar
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia spoke Friday at a session of the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum.

item.title: Infant Killed as Israeli Military Fires on Car in West Bank, Palestinian Officials Say
item.description: Israel’s military said a soldier opened fire after Israeli troops “perceived a vehicle accelerating toward them.” The baby’s grandmother, who was in the car, disputed that account.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.media:description: Fahd Abu Haykal during the funeral of his 7-month-old son, Sam, in Hebron in the Israeli-occupied West Bank on Saturday.

item.title: A Viral Gen-Z Protest Movement Draws Thousands to India’s Capital
item.description: The Cockroach Janta Party started as a joke, but it quickly became a way for many Indians to express their frustration with the system.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das and Suhasini Raj

item.title: Israeli Strike Kills 3 Lebanese Soldiers, Days After Truce Was Signed
item.description: Israel invaded Lebanon and occupied parts of the country to fight Hezbollah, an Iran-backed armed group, but its military offensive has drawn in others.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Smoke after a strike near Nabatieh, in southern Lebanon, on Saturday.

item.title: As Ebola Spreads in East Africa, Will China Step Up?
item.description: China is well positioned to help stop the deadly virus, and could move into a gap left by U.S. retreat.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo and Stephanie Nolen
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: The coffin of a young woman presumed to have died from Ebola was transported for burial in the town of Mongbwalu, the epicenter of the Ebola epidemic, in May.

item.title: What Visual Evidence Tells Us About Israel’s Use of White Phosphorus in Lebanon
item.description: Videos collected by The Times shows how the Israeli military has deployed a munition that can be extremely harmful over populated areas in Lebanon.
item.dc:creator: Sanjana Varghese
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: A photo taken by The Associated Press on Nov. 22, 2023, shows white phosphorus charges dispersing over Kfar Kila, a town in the south of Lebanon.

item.title: Mali Conflict Escalates as Rebel Alliance Seizes Key Cities
item.description: Jihadists have nearly tripled their attacks on urban areas. Analysts fear an extended standoff could lead to many more civilian deaths.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean
item.media:description: A soldier from the Azawad Liberation Front, one of the groups in a powerful insurgent alliance fighting Mali’s ruling junta, inside a damaged building in Kidal, Mali, last month.

item.title: Laboring Under Delhi’s Harsh Heat, Workers Must Choose Health or Wages
item.description: Severe heat waves have been hitting India since April, forcing many of the country’s essential workers to make tough decisions.
item.dc:creator: Pragati K.B., Anupreeta Das and Anindito Mukherjee
item.media:description: Vendors setting up large umbrellas to shade their kiosks last month at a flea market near Jama Masjid, a famous mosque in New Delhi.

item.title: The Gold Mines at the Heart of This Ebola Outbreak
item.description: Mining has been the lifeblood of this remote Congolese hill town for decades. Now, it is fueling the spread of a devastating virus.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh and Arlette Bashizi
item.category: Islamic State in Iraq and Syria (ISIS)
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times

item.title: What to Know About the Ebola Outbreak
item.description: Aid agencies are racing to help health workers in the Democratic Republic of Congo. The virus is known to have killed at least 84, but the true toll may be far higher.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Amelia Nierenberg and Ephrat Livni
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Disinfecting a path last month outside a hospital in Mongbwalu, a mining town at the center of the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: Backed by Trump, Opposed by Putin, and Fighting for His Political Life
item.description: Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan of Armenia is seeking re-election as his country weighs questions of war and peace, of autocracy and democracy, and of subjugation and independence.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne and Emile Ducke
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times

item.title: Despite Protest, Ye Takes the Stage for Thousands of European Fans
item.description: Nearly 40,000 fans came out to hear the rapper formerly known as Kanye West in the Netherlands on Saturday, even as other European countries had canceled his concerts.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Mouneb Taim/Associated Press, via Ap Photo/Mouneb Taim
item.media:description: “This crowd is too crazy,” Ye said inside the GelreDome stadium in Arnhem, the Netherlands, on Saturday.

item.title: Bernadette Chirac, Formidable Ex-First Lady of France, Dies at 93
item.description: Long seen as the cool, coifed wife of the president, she emerged as a political player in her own right, as well as a relentless champion of charities.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:description: Bernadette Chirac in 2006.

item.title: C.I.A. Officer Found With Gold Bars Said to Have Created Fake Spy Program
item.description: A federal judge has ruled that David Rush, the C.I.A. employee, must remain in detention. U.S. officials say Mr. Rush funneled millions in federal funds to himself.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Edward Wong
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: The George Bush Center for Intelligence at the Central Intelligence Agency in Langley, Va.

item.title: Trump’s Defense Department Sees Growing Espionage Threat From Israel
item.description: The Defense Department has increased the counterintelligence threat assessment to its highest level, and Israel is believed to have eavesdropped on American negotiations with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu last December. The United States and Israel were largely aligned at the war’s outset, with Mr. Trump endorsing Mr. Netanyahu’s goal to oust the Iranian government.

item.title: Balancing Act
item.description: The tension between sticking to what we know and seeking out new experiences is one of comfort versus novelty. How can we have both?
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: The World Cup Comes to Canada. But Does Anyone Care?
item.description: Staggering ticket prices and lackluster matches have left thousands of seats in Toronto and Vancouver unsold just days before the tournament kicks off.
item.dc:creator: Shawna Richer
item.media:description: BMO Field in Toronto will be known as Toronto Stadium for the World Cup.

item.title: The World Has Learned From the Last Ebola Outbreak, but Gaps Remain
item.description: The vaccine development process and coordination between health organizations have improved since a devastating 2014-2016 outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Nolen
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A health worker takes the temperature of a motorcyclist passing through a checkpoint to enter the city of Goma, Democratic Republic of Congo, as part of the response to Ebola.

item.title: Iran’s Soccer Team Allowed Into U.S. for World Cup, but Many Staff Denied
item.description: After months of doubts over their participation, Iran’s players have received visas for the United States just days before the World Cup begins.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Members of Iran’s national soccer team training in Antalya, Turkey, on Tuesday.

item.title: Pamela Hicks, Lady-in-Waiting to Elizabeth II of Britain, Dies at 97
item.description: The queen’s third cousin, she was a bridesmaid at the royal wedding in 1947, and witnessed firsthand pivotal moments in British history.
item.dc:creator: Alan Cowell
item.category: Elizabeth II, Queen of Great Britain

item.title: A Sherpa Survived 6 Days Alone on Everest. His Family Says He Was Abandoned.
item.description: Dawa Sherpa, 57, was found alive on Thursday, nearly a week after he was last seen on the mountain. His wife says more could have been done to find him sooner.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai and Binod Ghimire
item.media:credit: Prabin Ranabhat/Agence France-Presse, via Getty Images

item.title: France Opens Investigation Into Israeli Treatment of Pro-Palestinian Flotilla Activists
item.description: Activists who were detained with a flotilla trying to reach the Gaza Strip have said they were abused while in Israel’s custody. Israeli authorities have denied mistreating them.
item.dc:creator: Alan Yuhas
item.media:description: French activists from the Global Sumud Flotilla, who were detained by Israeli forces before they could reach the Gaza Strip, arriving in Paris in May.

item.title: A Question Swirling Around Putin’s Big Conference: Could the War End?
item.description: At the St. Petersburg economic forum, business and political elites said Russia faced a choice of halting the conflict or sacrificing more. Vladimir Putin seemed to signal more war.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia speaking at the International Economic Forum in St. Petersburg on Friday.

item.title: Crown Princess Mette-Marit of Norway Is Placed on Lung Transplant List
item.description: News of Crown Princess Mette-Marit’s worsening condition comes as a number of scandals have plunged the royal family into a deep crisis.
item.dc:creator: Henrik Pryser Libell and Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: Lise Åserud/NTB Scanpix, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Crown Princess Mette-Marit of Norway, center, with Crown Prince Haakon, right, and Prince Sverre Magnus at the National Day celebrations in Asker, Norway, last month.

item.title: Russian Satellites Have Been Jamming GPS Signals Across Europe, Scientists Say
item.description: Scientists and U.S. military briefers have linked short, widespread interference incidents to Russia, revealing vulnerabilities in a technology essential to everyday society.
item.dc:creator: Riley Mellen and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A cell tower in Moeuvres, France.

item.title: Hopes of Lebanon Cease-Fire Falter as Israel and Hezbollah Fight On
item.description: The U.S.-brokered agreement requires Hezbollah, which Iran backs, to stop firing first. But the group, not party to the talks, rejected the conditions as a virtual surrender.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Smoke billowing from an Israeli strike near Nabatieh in southern Lebanon on Friday.

item.title: How Ebola Spreads Through Gold Mining
item.description: Our chief Africa correspondent, Declan Walsh, reports from the epicenter of the Ebola outbreak on how the gold mining industry and the outbreak are linked.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh, Estelle Caswell, Coleman Lowndes and Arlette Bashizi

item.title: Former Prince Andrew Sublet Cottages on Royal Property Where He Lived Rent-Free
item.description: Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor received income from renting out three cottages at Royal Lodge, where he paid no rent himself, a British public spending watchdog found.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor lived at Royal Lodge, an estate in Windsor, until February, when he was ousted after new revelations emerged about his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: Europe Needs New Military Alliance to Defend Itself, Italy Says
item.description: Guido Crosetto, Italy’s defense minister, said in an interview that European nations should forge a new joint defense system and take greater responsibility for their own security.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Italian marines during a military exercise in the Arctic Circle in 2024.

item.title: On Patrol With Tokyo’s New Litter Police
item.description: Visitor numbers to Shibuya are surging and trash cans are scarce. Enter the city patrol.
item.dc:creator: Kiuko Notoya
item.media:description: On the streets of Shibuya, patrol teams caught 10 people littering on the first day of the new ordinance, 15 on the second day and 9 on the third, according to city officials.

item.title: Russia Floods Armenia With Disinformation Ahead of Election
item.description: Fearing a loss of influence, groups linked to the Kremlin and intelligence agencies have sought to discredit the country’s prime minister.
item.dc:creator: Steven Lee Myers
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: A campaign event last week for Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan ahead of Armenian parliamentary elections.

item.title: Ye Concerts Have Been Canceled Across Europe. Why Not in the Netherlands?
item.description: Lawmakers and Jewish groups lodged protests over the rapper’s history of antisemitism. But those sensitivities ran headlong into the Netherlands’ robust free speech protections.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses

item.title: The Priests Revered in the Land of Five Genders
item.description: The bissus of South Sulawesi are considered a link between the earthly and celestial because they are thought to embody both male and female traits.
item.dc:creator: Sui-Lee Wee, Hasya Nindita and Nyimas Laula
item.media:description: Every year, the bissu in the Indonesian village of Segeri lead a ritual before the rice planting season. This procession, last November, included a sacred plow.

item.title: Russia Rails Against the West but Welcomes Candace Owens and Andrew Tate
item.description: While some powerful Russians shun the West, others want to restore ties and embrace friendly Westerners. President Vladimir V. Putin’s annual economic conference illustrates the conflicting impulses.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:description: A screen with an image of President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia at the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum on Wednesday.

item.title: What To Know as China’s Xi Jinping Heads to North Korea
item.description: As Xi Jinping visits Pyongyang, he faces an emboldened North Korean dictator, whose alliance with Russia has reduced his dependence on China.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson and Choe Sang-Hun
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:description: Xi Jinping, China’s leader, with North Korea’s leader, Kim Jong-un, in Beijing in September. Analysts say China is likely keen to assert its influence over North Korea as Pyongyang has leaned toward Russia.

item.title: House Passes Ukraine Aid in Defiance of Republican Leaders
item.description: Eighteen G.O.P. lawmakers broke with their party and joined Democrats to deliver yet another blow to the president’s foreign policy agenda.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:description: Ukrainian soldiers in the Donetsk region of eastern Ukraine in January. The House bill passed Thursday included $8 billion in loans to Ukraine and $1.8 billion in aid.

item.title: Zelensky Mixes Taunts and Peace Talks Offer in Letter to Putin
item.description: “After 26 years in power, age is beginning to take its toll,” President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine wrote of his Russian counterpart, bragging of a recent strike on St. Petersburg.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine, speaking in Sweden last week.

item.title: Tennis Giants Tumble
item.description: All the big names are out at the French Open. The result is a very confusing, and very exciting, tournament.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: Nose Gear on Boeing 787-9 Dreamliner Collapses, Injuring Several Workers
item.description: The airline Lufthansa said the cause of the accident at Frankfurt Airport was under investigation. The plane can weigh 279 tons at takeoff.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:description: Crew members who were preparing the plane ahead of a flight to Los Angeles were injured.

item.title: As Trump Toughens Rules on Cuba’s Economy, Hotel Chains Withdraw
item.description: The Trump administration’s efforts to tighten the economic noose on Cuba appear to be working, as more international firms announced they would leave the island.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:description: The Catedral in Havana is one of 15 hotels in Cuba run by the Spanish firm, Meliá.

item.title: Wary of U.S., Carney Bets on AI Strategy for Canada
item.description: The country on Thursday released a national artificial intelligence strategy that focuses on building its sovereign capability and protecting consumers.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Chris Young/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada during a demonstration at a Toronto hospital on Thursday. “Canadian A.I. adoption will be prudent, pragmatic and pro-worker,” he said.

item.title: Kuwait releases videos showing the moment a drone struck its airport.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kulish and James McManagan

item.title: Mistrust Spreads With the Ebola Virus in Congo
item.description: In a remote mining town at the center of the Democratic Republic of Congo’s Ebola outbreak, grief and mistrust are complicating efforts to stop the virus. Health workers are trying to treat the ill and bury the dead, but some residents are still in denial that the disease is even real.
item.dc:creator: Michael Anthony Adams, Bethlehem Feleke, Yasu Tsuji and Jon Hazell

item.title: Latest Israel-Lebanon Cease-Fire Shows Little Sign of Taking Hold
item.description: The U.S.-brokered deal depends on Hezbollah halting its attacks first. But the leader of the Iran-backed group rejected those terms, and Israel said that its offensive would continue.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.media:description: Israeli airstrikes in southern Lebanon on Thursday.

item.title: How One Company Plans to Mine the Bottom of the Sea
item.description: A look at the quest to mine the bottom of the ocean for valuable minerals, the connection between light pollution and allergies and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: Harry Stevens

item.title: Ultra-Orthodox Riot Shocks Israelis In Latest Protest At Military Draft
item.description: Israel’s Haredim have escalated their protests against military service in recent weeks, underscoring growing divisions ahead of national elections.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner

item.title: Tracking the Rising Death Toll From U.S. Boat Strikes
item.description: The New York Times has been investigating the number of boat strikes and the death toll in the Caribbean and Eastern Pacific carried out by the Trump administration. Eric Schmitt, a national security correspondent for The Times, explains what those numbers mean.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt, Gilad Thaler, Nikolay Nikolov, Shawn Paik, Rafaela Balster, Stephanie Swart, Lazaro Gamio and Whitney Shefte
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )

item.title: Fighting in Somalia’s Capital, Mogadishu: What’s Happening and Why It Matters
item.description: Gun battles erupted on Thursday, as armed groups loyal to rival politicians clashed. Residents said it was the worst fighting in years.
item.dc:creator: Hussein Mohamed and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.media:description: Government forces patrolling on Thursday in a district of Mogadishu, the capital of Somalia.

item.title: Hezbollah’s Fiber-Optic Drones Expose Cracks in Israeli Defenses
item.description: The Lebanese militant group’s attacks have caught Israelis off guard, forcing its political and military leaders to scramble for solutions.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon and Natan Odenheimer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Israeli soldiers reacting to drones near the Israel-Lebanon border on Monday.

item.title: Marjane Satrapi, Artist and Author of ‘Persepolis,’ Dies at 56
item.description: Her popular graphic novel series, published in the early 2000s, followed an Iranian girl through the Islamic Revolution and the Iran-Iraq War.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: Marjane Satrapi in 2022. She was one of the best-known exponents of a form of graphic novel that combined political history and memoir.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward, Ephrat Livni and Max Bearak

item.title: Can Abelardo De La Espriella, a U.S. Citizen, Become Colombia’s President?
item.description: President Trump this week endorsed the right-wing candidate, Abelardo De La Espriella, a dual citizen of Colombia and the United States, in Colombia’s presidential runoff.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:description: Abelardo De La Espriella with his ballot after voting in the first round of the presidential election in Barranquilla, Colombia, on Sunday.

item.title: Priest’s Ebola Death Stirs Fear and Doubt in Congolese Town
item.description: Priest’s Ebola Death Stirs Fear an Doubt in Congolese Town
item.dc:creator: Arlette Bashizi
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times

item.title: Colombia’s Yellow World Cup Jersey, Once a Symbol of Unity, Becomes a Political Statement
item.description: A presidential candidate endorsed by President Trump has been accused by some Colombians of co-opting their beloved national team’s yellow soccer jersey.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supporters of Abelardo de la Espriella, a right-wing presidential candidate, in Medellín, Colombia, last month.

item.title: Urban Light Pollution Might Be Worsening Allergies
item.description: Light pollution prompts plants to shed pollen longer and stronger, according to new research.
item.dc:creator: Marta Zaraska
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: A study found that trees in cities with brighter lights start producing pollen earlier in the spring and finish later in the fall, adding up to 130 days per year to the allergy season.

item.title: Ireland, Seen as a Weak Link in Europe’s Defense, Is Trying to Bulk Up
item.description: As concern rises in Europe over threats from an emboldened Russia, the Irish government says it’s working to plug gaps in its military, which reflect a tradition of neutrality.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia and Paulo Nunes dos Santos

item.title: Four New Zealand Lawmakers Banned From China After Taiwan Trip
item.description: New Zealand’s foreign minister was “surprised” to hear about the one-year travel ban, a spokesperson said, given lawmakers have visited Taiwan for years without issues.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Presidential Office of Taiwan, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Vice President Bi-khim Hsiao of Taiwan, in red, posing for a group photo with, from left to right, the New Zealand lawmakers Laura McClure, Maureen Pugh, Duncan Webb and David Wilson at the presidential office in Taipei, Taiwan, in May.
